office_does: A State Representative serves in Washington's 98-member House, writing and voting on state laws and the operating, capital, and transportation budgets, including K-12 funding, the state tax structure, housing mandates, and public-safety statutes.
race_blurb: Appointed incumbent Zach Hall (D) faces Republican police chief Michelle Bennett, Democrat Aimee Warmerdam running to Hall's left, and no-party candidate Topher Leritz in a district Democrats hold but Republicans view as contestable. Taxes and public safety are the most documented contrasts in the field.

candidates.summary: Zach Hall (D) is the appointed incumbent, a former six-year Issaquah City Councilmember and Eastside Fire & Rescue Board chair who passed two bipartisan bills in his first legislative session.
highlights: Sponsored and passed HB 2296 (utility-financed home energy-efficiency upgrades) and HB 2575 (streamlined environmental reporting) in his first session [S3]
Voted for the new millionaire-earner income tax enacted this year [S5]
Six years on the Issaquah City Council and chaired the Eastside Fire & Rescue Board before appointment to the House [S1]
Endorsed by Gov. Ferguson, Rep. Schrier, WA Labor Council, WA Education Association, and Eastside Firefighters [S2]
Largest war chest in the race: $44,039 across 86 contributions [S6]

taxes.basis: Voted for the new millionaire-earner income tax; the dossier's sharpest documented tax-policy data point for him.

spending.basis: Supports fully funding public schools and expanding childcare, healthcare, and behavioral-health services — an invest-and-expand agenda.

climate.basis: Environment & Energy vice chair who passed a clean-energy-efficiency financing bill and is endorsed by WA Conservation Action, while framing energy policy around lowering costs.

experience.basis: Incumbent with two passed bills, six years on city council, fire-board chairmanship, and prior legislative-staff work — a proven-record profile.

reform.basis: Runs with the field's broadest institutional endorsement slate (governor, labor, party figures) and largest war chest, working within existing party structures.

healthcare.basis: Supports expanding access to affordable healthcare and behavioral-health services within the existing system, not single-payer replacement.

social.basis: Endorsed by Planned Parenthood Alliance Advocates, indicating alignment with reproductive-rights positions.

candidates.summary: Michelle Bennett (R) is Mercer Island's police chief with a 35-year law-enforcement career including chief posts in Maple Valley, Sammamish, and Mercer Island, and holds a Doctorate in Education.
highlights: Opposes an income tax and calls the recent state budget "the largest tax increase in state history" [S1]
Says police "have been left without clear tools to protect victims and neighborhoods" and runs on her three decades as a police chief in the district [S1]
Pledges to ensure state spending delivers measurable results [S1]
Second-highest fundraising in the race: $21,352 across 81 contributions [S5]

taxes.basis: Opposes an income tax and criticizes the recent budget as the largest tax increase in state history — explicit in both pamphlet and news coverage.

spending.basis: Pledges to ensure state spending delivers measurable results, framing her candidacy around respect for taxpayers.

safety.basis: Says police lack clear tools to protect victims and neighborhoods; runs on a 35-year enforcement career with multiple chief posts.

experience.basis: Deep institutional record as a law-enforcement executive, but a first-time candidate with no legislative record — mixed on record vs. renewal.

candidates.summary: Aimee Warmerdam (D) is a first-time candidate and grassroots organizer with Seattle DSA and Whole Washington, running to the incumbent's left on a single-payer and social-housing program.
highlights: Supports single-payer universal healthcare via the Whole Washington initiative [S1]
Champions a $26 minimum wage, a 32-hour workweek, a millionaire's tax, and a payroll tax on large companies [S2]
Supports new social housing, multi-family zoning, and tiny-house villages for homeless residents [S1]
Refuses corporate PAC money; smallest verified fundraising among the Democrats at $3,679 [S4]
Opposes AI data-center expansion on energy and environmental grounds [S2]

taxes.basis: Explicitly backs a millionaire's tax, a payroll tax on large companies, and progressive taxation of the ultra-rich to fund her program.

spending.basis: Program of major public expansion: fully funded schools, free school meals, tuition-free community college, expanded transit, and a state bank.

housing.basis: Centers social housing, anti-speculation measures, and tiny-house villages, while also supporting streamlined permitting and multi-family zoning.

climate.basis: Would reverse cuts to the Department of Natural Resources and opposes AI data-center expansion on energy and environmental grounds.

experience.basis: First-time legislative candidate with no office beyond PCO, running on organizing background and lived working-class experience.

reform.basis: Refuses corporate PAC money and runs against the "corporate duopoly," with minimal institutional fundraising.

healthcare.basis: Supports single-payer universal healthcare via the Whole Washington initiative — the only such candidate in the race.

tech.basis: Opposes AI data-center expansion on energy and environmental grounds, a limits-oriented posture toward the industry.

candidates.summary: Topher Leritz states no party preference and runs an explicitly nonpartisan campaign, accepting no donations or endorsements; his background is in marketing, political consulting, and private investigation.
highlights: Accepts zero endorsements or special-interest donations as a matter of principle [S1]
Criticizes current leadership for focusing on "reelection campaigns, political outrage, and national talking points" over local problems [S1]
Emphasizes affordable housing, public safety paired with mental-health services, and accountability [S2]

spending.basis: Emphasizes accountability and practical results from government rather than program expansion.

safety.basis: Pairs improved public-safety services with mental-health services, without leaning toward enforcement or prevention poles.

experience.basis: No elected experience; self-described "not a career politician" running as an outsider to both parties.

reform.basis: Refuses all donations and endorsements — zero PDC-reported contributions — making anti-special-interest practice his core differentiator.
